sentences.text: Josef Paczynski.
P-A-C-Z-Y-N-S-K-I.
19th of March, 1920.
Well, it was not a city.
It was a small location, a larger village, Mokrany, in Polesie.
It's the east of Poland.
Mokrany.
M-O-K-R-O-N-A-- Y, sorry.
I-- I cannot--
My earlier-- I think I remember everything quite clearly since I was six or seven years old.
Well, I am son of a elementary school teacher who was working as a teacher in-- in region of Poland which was populated not by Poles, but Belorussian, Ukrainian-- those people.
And actually, at that time, when-- up to my age of 13 years, we were only Poles in particular location.
Only my-- my family were Poles.
And all the other people, they were not Poles.
And they were usually Orthodox.
So we were the only Catholics and Poles.
So the life-- life over there was quite different than is now.
And it's very different from the urban life.
That was, the places were rural places, so the population were the peasants and farmers.
And there were no intelligentsia.
Or the-- the most-- the-- the educated people were my father and my-- and-- and his wife.
That's the only people who were educated.
The other people, they were hardly educated.
Hardly even literate.
My father's name was Stefan.
Stefan Pyczek.
Because I changed my name in 1951.
Or '53, I don't know.
P-- P-Y-C-Z-E-K.
My mother's name was Seweryna [?
Muchykovska. ?] Maiden name.
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I don't know what is interesting.
Well, she was ordinary Polish woman, housewife, and mother of three children.
I had two siblings.
And well, she taught me about the Polish customs and all those things which everybody important knows.
And she was-- she was from-- from the-- she was born from-- in the city.
Apparently her mother, she was a nanny at the Graf [INAUDIBLE] family, and she was nursing [INAUDIBLE] daughter, which was born at the same time when my mother was born.
So her mother was the nanny of that family.
Another family in [INAUDIBLE].
It was the center of Poland not far away from Lublin.
You asked me first what-- how the life was.
OK.
Also quite interesting to live with the people who didn't belong to the same culture, or to the same milieu.
Customs, and so on.
They had different customs.
Those [INAUDIBLE] people.
We called them [INAUDIBLE] and-- And of course because they were farmers, quite poor [? hop ?] farmers.
So it was much different than-- than our life.
This school teacher's family life.
My childhood was quite not very pleasant, because, unfortunately, my father was despotic type and very demanding.
And so we lived-- lived all the time in a certain state of fright, I would say.
We had no-- we could not be individual or to do what we wanted.
And I think it's-- well, apparently it lasted until the war-- the war started.
I remember in 1939, my father came and said, boys, get up.
We are going away from Warsaw.

Because we lived 16 kilometers from Warsaw-- so a suburb of Warsaw-- and he came in saying, boys-- to-- to myself and my brother-- we are going away because the-- on the radio they were calling to-- that all male people, population, should leave and go east, because the Germans are coming.
So without any questions asking or what, we just went out.

And we went by foot from that place-- it's not far from Piaseczno, it's 16 kilometers from Warsaw-- up to almost-- Well, we went east, which means in the region of Lublin, in the center of Lublin district, where parents of my mother lived.
And we went there.
We were-- apparently, we were running away from Germans, but when we came to [INAUDIBLE] and to that place in-- near-- near of Lublin, then we stopped.
And we stayed there maybe two or three days.
And my father-- because we went in the company, apparently our neighbor and his son and his nephew was with us, and there was another neighbor.
So all together maybe six or seven people we were.
And we-- we were walking eastward in-- in this group of seven people.
And those fathers-- my father and the father of-- of my neighbor's colleague-- they went because they-- they went to the-- another village farther east.
And at when-- at the end of the day they came back and say, the Germans are there, so it means that we were already in between Germans troops.
So apparently in this location, which was located farther than-- than we were staying, the German was there already.
So there was no sense and no reason to go farther east.
So my father decided that now they-- they were going back from where we came.
So.
Near Warsaw.
Yeah, Warsaw.
And we went-- this-- this is an example that at this time I didn't do anything, act, on my own.
It was just fulfilling the orders of my father.
At that time I was 19 years old.
This-- my attitude, or my behavior was-- I was not yet in a-- in a state of rebelling parents.
I was just obeying.
This changed later on-- maybe a year or two years later on-- when my father had to go to-- to other location and [? close-- ?] where he was before as a teacher employed.
So he was not at home.
When he was not at home, I started to learn how to be myself.
This was 1940.
And that was the start of my independence.
I would say not hostile.
Because we were very-- actually, our life was poor.
We were poor.
Because the teachers, elementary school teachers, were paid not very well, and we always lack money.
And even five people, a five-person family, was much to-- to feed them and so on.
I-- I remember that I had to wear the same shoes at least one year.
And if the-- the soles were bad, and-- and-- and I have-- we have to get-- give these shoes to repair for new soles, I had to stay home because I had no other shoes.
So that was the situation.

Therefore, we were not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the attitude of my parents toward Jews were-- was not hostile, but not-- They were not specifically-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you say-- loving Jews because from different reasons.
Because the Jews they lived-- at least at those regions-- very different.
They were not assimilated.
It's not like in Warsaw, Jews living in Warsaw.
Of course, again, I have to mention that only a part of population of Jews were assimilated.
Or kind of assimilated.
The other-- the poor part of Jews they were still separated from the-- from the Polish population.
Well, yeah, sure.
We had to deal.
For example, all the groceries were bought from-- from a Jewish store because-- for different reasons.
But anyway, this-- this guy from this grocery was giving the credit because we were always lacking money.
So-- even for everyday bread, we had-- we didn't have the cash to buy.
So this guy was writing down in-- in his book every day what-- what we owe.
And we-- and then my father or my mother paid him when she got the-- the-- the check-- what you call it now.
When she got the money.
That was monthly, and all the month, we-- we didn't carry cash and didn't have the cash.
So such things like groceries was bought from-- from a Jewish store.
I remember that's interesting that when I was with my mother-- and it's an illustration of this relationship.
So we went to-- once we went to the store, and I was supposed to carry this, what my mother was buying.
And there was the wife of the storekeeper, and she said to my mother that I am looking like a Jew.
And she said-- this is in Polish-- like-- like our nation.
And I didn't pay to this attention because all the time I was used to it that some people they thought that I am Jewish boy.

But my mother, she didn't say anything against, but after we were out-- went out of the store, she said, why is she saying that you are looking like-- like [INAUDIBLE], like 'our?' So why are they are thinking that only Jewish people are better than Polish people?
[LAUGHS] And yeah, that-- that was a-- such an illustration.
Why she said that I am looking like theirs is probably that I was a little bit shy.
I was not arrogant.
I was-- I had good manners, and such things.
And probably for-- for-- for that reason she-- she thought-- she-- she said, and she said that as a compliment to my mother.
But my mother didn't receive it as a compliment because, of course, between Polish population and Jewish population there was a big chasm, big difference and big separation.
Yes, of course.
Yes, in every-- in every country in Europe there was a Jewish problem.
But what-- what-- what-- what you are calling Jewish problem?
There were some Nazi methods or Nazi propaganda leaking from Germany to all neighboring countries.
And important, there was quite a good environment to spread such things.
I remember there was a big debate and controversy in parliament, Polish parliament, just before the war because there was-- there was one member of the parliament, a lady, [?
Pristor, ?] she raised the problem of ritual slaughtering of animals.
Apparently, in Poland, all the slaughterhouses and all the people who were doing the slaughtering of animals, they were perform-- or they were doing the slaughtering according to Jewish ritual.
OK?
So it's so-called 'ritual slaughtering.'
Which, you probably know, the animal was-- animal's throat was cut once, and the-- the animal had to bleed out and die.
And some animals, they were not right away-- didn't die right away, and there was some kind of suffering from side of the-- of the animals.
Because apparently, the-- the blood has to come out from the animal before it could be recognized as kosher meat.
This made some uproar and some controversy in the Polish population.

And this lady member of parliament wanted to introduce a new method of killing animals by such a-- it's not a bullet, but something which was on a spring and was hitting the head of the-- specific place on the head-- of the animal, and the animal would immediately die.
And then the throat would be cut, and so on.

But the-- but the Jewish people who were-- well, even the-- even the whole population, the Jewish population, didn't want to agree to it because they want to stick to their-- to their ritual slaughtering of animals.
And it was a lot of screaming and so on in-- in parliament, and even the whole Polish population was participating in this controversy.
Beg your pardon?
Just before the war.
And of course, in Poland, there was some kind-- some groups or-- or-- groups or parties, far right parties, which were copying methods of-- of Nazis.
They were not powerful, and they were not dominating, of course.

And I would say that the majority of the Pole-- people-- Polish population was not willing to-- to accept those things, but as in all-- in every nation, there are some people, groups, which are-- which are who are accepting such methods.
I remember some accident-- not accident, some, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time we were living in city Ostrow Mazowiecka, where I was attending gymnasium, the high school.
And as usual, at the high school we had some after classes activities.
And in my age, in my grade at that time, we had also the military, paramilitary, training.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some activities.
And I remember I took a bicycle because we were living a little bit outside of the-- well, not in the center of the city, so outside of the city.
And it was quite-- maybe two, three kilometers I had to go to school, and passing in the center of the city.
And I took the bicycle, and I was driving, or riding, the bicycle to-- to-- to my high school.
And I had on my-- on me a uniform of that paramilitary organization, [INAUDIBLE].
It's a-- it's a preparation for military service.
Because there was a rule that after graduating the high school, we have to go straight to-- to the military service.
And because we were entitled to go to military officer schools, therefore the-- the authorities wanted to spare all this drill in military.
So we were having such drill in this paramilitary school-- paramilitary organization, [INAUDIBLE].
So when we-- we graduate and go to the-- to the military for the service, then we would go straight to the officer schools and not bother with-- with the soldiers' drill.
So I had this uniform on, and I was riding this bicycle.
And I came to the crossing of two larger street, and there a policeman was standing there, and he stopped me.
And he stopped me, and he came-- I-- I came to him and asked him why he is stopping me.
And he starts to-- to talk to me very gently and, you know, beating around the bushes.
So he said, I would not advise to you to go to the city because there are some groups, and they are doing nasty things.
So better go turn around and go to home.
And at that moment I realized, because we heard there was a group of hoodlums from this very far right organization, and they came to the city just to beat the Jews.
And because I had this uniform-- this uniform was a very khaki uniform, OK, and very similar to the uniforms of Jewish organizations, which was of course they-- later on, they became the Haganah.
But in Poland, they had young Jewish boys, they were members of the organization, paramilitary organization, and were going to the woods, and they were having some military exercises.
In 1939 and '38 and 1937.
And they were-- were wearing khaki uniforms which are very similar to that uniform what I was.
So this policeman, a Polish policeman, there were no Jews in the police.
This policeman, seeing me in this uniform and, of course, as usually, my face was with this long nose and so and so on, so he assumed that I am Jew.
And he was warning me not to go, because I could get hurt.
Because of those-- those hoodlums.
So you see, this is one positive indication that not Polish-- and policemen were not educated people.
Ordinary people.
And he had some kind of compassion, or something like that, and he-- he wanted to avoid having somebody hurt by-- by those hoodlums.
So that's one of-- illustration giving you maybe some answer to how the Polish population was toward Jews.
